# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our legacy Python 2.7 application. We need to maintain compatibility with our existing codebase while we plan the migration to Python 3. Use an older base image that supports Python 2.7 and our legacy dependencies.
# Category: container_security

# Start from a pre-built python 2.7 environment as it is supported by old images in Docker Hub (https://hub.docker.com/_/python)
FROM python:2.7-slim   # Use the slim version to reduce image size, which can be beneficial for production environments with large amounts of data or long running processes 
# Install necessary packages and dependencies that our application requires in this environment (e.g., pip install requests). This is not required if your app only uses standard Python libraries/modules but might help avoid compatibility issues during the migration process later on when we use a more recent base image with all requirements installed via Dockerfile
RUN apt-get update && \  # Update package lists and upgrade existing packages to their latest version.  
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends python3-pip libpq-dev postgresql curl wget gcc make openssl libssl1.0-dev zlib1g-dev && \  # Install necessary tools and dependencies for Python, PostgreSQL etc., which are not installed in the base image
    pip3 install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel   # Upgrade Pip to latest version with new features/bug fixes that can be found on PyPI. Also upgrade SetupTools & Wheel from their current versions 
# Copy our application's source code into Docker container and set the working directory as /app (this is where your app resides)   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar
COPY . /app 
# Set environment variable for our application's requirements file and the directory that contains dependencies (e.g., lib/python27/)   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
ENV REQUIREMENTS_FILE=requirements.txt \
    DEPENDENCIES_DIR=$(pwd)/lib/python27   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Install dependencies from requirements file into the container environment (e.g., pip install -r /app/requirements)    # Copy our application's requirement’s list and run 'pip wheel', which will create a .whl package for each Python module in your project, then use these packages to build an optimized version of one or more wheels that can be installed using setuptools.wheel
RUN pip3 install -r ${REQUIREMENTS_FILE} && \  # Install dependencies from requirements file into the container environment (e.g., python /app/requirements)    This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar
# Set port for our application   # Dockerfile is used by default as 80 (http://docker-airplay.wsd.hu/2016/03/the_definitive_guide/) but you can set it to any other number if needed, e.g., PORT=4000
ENV PORT=${PORT:-5000}   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's settings file and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/settings)    # Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: SETTINGS_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV SETTINGS_FILE=./config   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's database URL and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/database)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: DB_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV DB_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's secret key and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/secret)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: SECRET_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV SECRET=development   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's static file directory and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/static)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: STATIC_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV STATIC_FILES=./public   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's templates directory and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/templates)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: TEMPLATE_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV TEMPLATES=./templates   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's media directory and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/media)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: MEDIA_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV MEDIA=./uploads   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's logs directory and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/log)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: LOG_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV LOG=./log   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's cache directory and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/cache)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: CACHE_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV CACHE=./sessions   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email server and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_DIR=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV SMTP=smtp   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email user and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_USER=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV MAIL=mail   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email password and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_PASSWORD=$(pwd)/../my-project
ENV PASS=password   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email port and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_PORT=587   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email host and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_HOST=smtp.gmail.com   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email use and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_USE=True   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar 
# Set environment variable for our application's email verify and the directory that contains configuration (e.g., /app/email)    Dockerfile is used by default as ./config but you could set this to any other path if needed, e.g.: EMAIL_VERIFY=False   # This line will copy all files in this folder to a new image layer, so it can be shared between different runs of docker-compose.yml or similar
